The simple LCAO-MO wavefunction already predicts a bound molecule, though it overestimates the bond length and underestimates the binding energy.

and ultimately by configuration interaction, which reaches essentially exact agreement with experiment.

children.value: The main difficulty in the molecular Hamiltonian is the

children.html: 1/r121/r_{12}1/r12​

children.value: term, which couples the two electrons to each other. This means that a simple product wavefunction is not sufficient. No analytic solution has been found for the electronic Schrodinger equation of

children.html: H2H_2H2​

children.value: For this reason, we solve the problem approximately by using the LCAO-MO approach used previously. For example, the ground state for

children.html: H2H_2H2​

children.value: is obtained by placing two electrons with opposite spins in the

children.html: 1σg1\sigma_g1σg​

children.value: orbital. This assumes that the wavefunction is expressed as an antisymmetrized product (a Slater determinant).
Constructing MOs for the hydrogen molecule

children.label: Constructing MOs for the hydrogen molecule

children.value: According to the Pauli principle, two electrons with opposite spins can be assigned to a given spatial orbital. As a first approximation, we assume that the molecular orbitals in

children.html: H2H_2H2​
H2+H_2^+H2+​

children.value: Previously we found that the total wavefunction must be antisymmetric with respect to exchange of electron indices. This can be achieved by using the Slater determinant:

children.value: Note that this wavefunction is only approximate and is definitely not an eigenfunction of the

children.html: H2H_2H2​

children.value: electronic Hamiltonian. Thus we must calculate the electronic energy by taking the expectation value of this wavefunction with the Hamiltonian (the actual calculation is not shown):

children.html: E(R)=2E1s+e24πϵ0R−integrals{E(R) = 2E_{1s} + \frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 R} - \textnormal{integrals}}E(R)=2E1s​+4πϵ0​Re2​−integrals
E1sE_{1s}E1s​

children.value: is the electronic energy of one hydrogen atom. The second term represents the Coulomb repulsion between the two positively charged nuclei, and the last term (“integrals”) contains a series of integrals describing the interactions of various charge distributions with one another (see P. W. Atkins, Molecular Quantum Mechanics, Oxford University Press). With this approach, the minimum energy is reached at

children.html: RRR

children.value: = 84 pm (experimental 74.1 pm) with a dissociation energy

children.value: Improving upon the simple MO approximation

children.label: Improving upon the simple MO approximation

children.html: H2H_2H2​

children.value: wavefunction by treating ionic and covalent contributions separately lowers the energy and shortens the predicted bond length toward experiment.
This simple approach is not very accurate, but it demonstrates that the method works. To improve the accuracy, ionic and covalent terms should be considered separately:

children.html: 1sA(1)1sA(2)⏟Ionic (H− + H+)+[1sA(1)1sB(2)+1sA(2)1sB(1)]⏟Covalent (H + H)+1sB(1)1sB(2)⏟Ionic (H+ + H−){\underbrace{1s_A(1)1s_A(2)}_{\textnormal{Ionic (H}^- \textnormal{ + H}^+)} + \underbrace{[1s_A(1)1s_B(2) + 1s_A(2)1s_B(1)]}_{\textnormal{Covalent (H + H)}} + \underbrace{1s_B(1)1s_B(2)}_{\textnormal{Ionic (H}^+ \textnormal{ + H}^-)}}Ionic (H− + H+)1sA​(1)1sA​(2)​​+Covalent (H + H)[1sA​(1)1sB​(2)+1sA​(2)1sB​(1)]​​+Ionic (H+ + H−)1sB​(1)1sB​(2)​​

children.value: Further improvement can be achieved by adding higher atomic orbitals to the wavefunction. The previously discussed Hartree-Fock method provides an efficient way of solving the problem. Recall that this method is only approximate, as it ignores electron-electron correlation effects completely. The full treatment requires configuration interaction methods, which can yield essentially exact results:
